claim: Winning in vertical AI is fundamentally an organizational problem, not a model sophistication problem
actionable: Prioritize hiring and organizing domain experts within your AI company structure rather than only optimizing model architectures
source_title: How to Leverage Domain Expertise — Chris Lovejoy, Notius Labs

claim: The Oracle pattern works best when AI quality is subjective (like meeting notes) and the output is small/reviewable at scale
actionable: Use the Oracle model (single expert directly improving prompts) when your product has no objective quality metrics and manageable output volume
source_title: How to Leverage Domain Expertise — Chris Lovejoy, Notius Labs

claim: The Evaluator pattern requires objective metrics and enables scale by defining quality standards that engineers can optimize against
actionable: Build objective metrics for quality (e.g., clinician reviews, LLM-as-judge systems) and hire domain experts to define what success looks like rather than fix individual outputs
source_title: How to Leverage Domain Expertise — Chris Lovejoy, Notius Labs

claim: The Architect pattern automates both quality assessment and improvement, requiring domain expertise to design systems that learn from usage
actionable: When manual iteration becomes a bottleneck, design automated improvement mechanisms (feedback loops, dynamic adaptation) rather than relying on human review cycles
source_title: How to Leverage Domain Expertise — Chris Lovejoy, Notius Labs

claim: Domain expertise should be 'direct experience of the use case' not just general credentials; medical coding expertise differs from general medical expertise
actionable: When hiring domain experts, ask about specific workflows and edge cases they've personally handled, not just their title or credentials
source_title: How to Leverage Domain Expertise — Chris Lovejoy, Notius Labs

claim: A single principal domain expert accountable for AI quality prevents slow consensus-by-committee and enables faster, better decisions
actionable: Designate one person (not a committee) as accountable for assessing and improving AI quality, giving them ownership and decision-making authority
source_title: How to Leverage Domain Expertise — Chris Lovejoy, Notius Labs

claim: Domain experts treated as advisors rather than decision-makers tend to leave after 12-18 months, taking valuable product context with them
actionable: Ensure domain experts have ownership, decision authority, and a seat at the table—not just review permissions
source_title: How to Leverage Domain Expertise — Chris Lovejoy, Notius Labs

claim: Hire domain experts with breadth of adjacent skills (prompting, statistics, leadership) rather than narrow expertise, allowing them to grow across Oracle→Evaluator→Architect roles
actionable: When hiring domain experts, look for multiple relevant skills and willingness to learn engineering/data science, so they can evolve with your product
source_title: How to Leverage Domain Expertise — Chris Lovejoy, Notius Labs

claim: About 50% of generative AI projects were abandoned (per Gartner) partly because teams lacked deep understanding of workflows being automated
actionable: Invest in understanding target workflows and hiring domain experts early to avoid abandoning projects at the last mile
source_title: How to Leverage Domain Expertise — Chris Lovejoy, Notius Labs

claim: Front-end models are now good enough; the real competitive gap in AI products is operationalizing expert judgment around model outputs
actionable: Stop optimizing for marginal model improvements; instead, focus on organizational systems that incorporate domain expertise into product development
source_title: How to Leverage Domain Expertise — Chris Lovejoy, Notius Labs
